Open /welcomeback

Teacher guide for /welcomeback

Teach Harness Engineering From the Neural Orchestrator Page

Use this while students look at mojoaistudio.com/welcomeback. It tells you what to point at, what concept it demonstrates, and what words to use live.

Open live page Run of show
Frame Run Page map Walkthrough Cheats Exercise

Correct teaching frame

This is not a generic harness lecture. The page is the harness artifact.

The goal is to teach harness engineering by walking through the actual command-center surface, modals, skill downloads, harness cards, and proof/gate language on `/welcomeback/`.

Opening script

Start by naming what students are looking at.

"This page is not just a class landing page. Treat it as a harness surface. It sets up the teaching context, organizes the work, exposes controls, links skills, shows dependencies, and gives us a way to inspect the system."

"Tonight, when I say harness, I do not only mean test harness. I mean a controlled wrapper around work: setup, run, observe, judge, and recover."

"The `/welcomeback` page is our working example of that wrapper."

90-minute class

Run of show tied to /welcomeback

Keep the live page open the whole time. Do not let the class become vocabulary-only. Every concept should point back to a visible page element.

0-10 min

Orient to the surface

Show the top bar, countdown/status, side nav, and repo links. Name them as setup and context.

10-25 min

Define harness

Use the page itself: it prepares, presents, controls, observes, and offers recovery paths.

25-45 min

Walk harness cards

Open Class_Agenda cards and the detail modal. Teach function, levers, and proposed skills.

45-65 min

Open build primer

Teach the nine-step system-design harness: work, stages, workers, contracts, handoffs, memory, tools, gates, tests.

65-90 min

Exercise and close

Students pick a visible harness card and translate it into setup, run, observe, judge, recover.

Page map

What each /welcomeback section teaches

Use this as your cheat sheet while screen-sharing. Each visible section maps to one harness concept.

Countdown and status

Harness setup and run state

The countdown makes the run bounded in time. The status label tells students whether the system is pre-run, in-run, or post-run.

Say: "A harness should make state visible before anyone starts clicking."

Valuable repositories

Dependencies and reference sources

Obsidianify and Graphify are not decoration. They are source systems the lesson uses to talk about memory, context, and repo understanding.

Say: "A harness names its dependencies instead of pretending they are implicit."

Class_Agenda cards

Harness inventory

The generated harness cards divide the orchestration system into understandable control surfaces.

Say: "This is an inventory of what has to be controlled before an agentic system is trustworthy."

Harness detail modal

Function, levers, and proof

Each modal explains the harness function and the knobs that move performance, cost, and capability.

Say: "A useful harness is tunable. If nothing can be adjusted, it is just a display."

Installable skills

Automation packaging

The SKILL.md downloads show how concepts become portable automation units that can be installed into a coding environment.

Say: "Development automation becomes useful when the capability can travel with the project."

Build primer modal

System-design harness

The nine collapsible steps are a harness for designing multi-agent systems without jumping straight to naming agents.

Say: "This modal protects the design process from premature implementation."

Live walkthrough

Exact teaching sequence on the page

Use these steps as your screen-share script. Each step has a visible action and a concept to say out loud.

1. Point to the page chrome

Top nav, side nav, countdown

Say: "Before a harness runs anything, it establishes where we are, what session we are in, and what state we are starting from."

  • Top nav equals stable global context.
  • Side nav equals related systems and reference destinations.
  • Countdown equals run boundary and status signal.
2. Point to Valuable Repositories

Dependencies and source-of-truth inputs

Say: "A harness should expose the sources it depends on. If those sources are hidden, students or developers cannot inspect the run."

  • Obsidianify demonstrates memory injection.
  • Graphify demonstrates repo graph understanding.
  • Other repositories are optional comparison context.
3. Open an Orchestrator card

Control plane harnesses

Say: "This layer is about the system around the work: routing, proof, session state, memory, tool permissions, release readiness."

  • Use Proof Authority to teach judgment.
  • Use Tool Registry to teach permission boundaries.
  • Use Session Manager to teach recovery.
4. Open a Model Harness card

Per-call boundary harnesses

Say: "Every model call is also a tiny harness: select, route, prompt, verify, trace, measure cost, and decide whether to trust it."

  • Use Prompt to teach controlled input.
  • Use Response to teach captured output.
  • Use Evidence and Trust to teach pass/fail.
5. Show performance/cost/capability

The three lever model

Say: "A harness is not only a wrapper. It gives us levers. We can tune speed, cost, or capability, but every lever has tradeoffs."

  • Performance levers change how fast the harness runs.
  • Cost levers limit waste and overuse.
  • Capability levers expand what the harness can safely do.
6. Open Comprehensive Build Primer

The design harness

Say: "This modal is a harness for thinking. It prevents us from building agents before we understand the work."

  • Step 1 is setup: define the work.
  • Steps 2-7 are run design: stages, workers, contracts, handoffs, memory, tools.
  • Steps 8-9 are judgment and recovery: gates, tests, observation, improvement.

Talk track

Verbiage to use while teaching the harness concepts

These lines are specific to `/welcomeback/`, so they should feel connected to the page on screen.

Setup

When showing the first viewport

Say: "Setup is not just installing packages. Setup is also orienting the operator. This page tells us what session we are in, what resources matter, and what controls are available."

Run

When opening cards and modals

Say: "The run here is interactive. We are not executing one shell command. We are walking a controlled teaching surface that reveals one subsystem at a time."

Observe

When discussing details and evidence

Say: "Observation means the harness makes hidden state visible: function, route, proof, cost, latency, policy, trace, and trust."

Judge

When using Proof Authority and Verify

Say: "A system is not trustworthy because it produced output. It becomes trustworthy when the harness knows how to judge the output."

Recover

When using Fallback and Session Manager

Say: "Recovery is designed before failure. Fallback, session lifecycle, rollback gates, and degraded behavior are recovery concepts."

Package

When using SKILL.md downloads

Say: "The moment a pattern becomes a skill file, it stops being just a lecture idea and starts becoming reusable development automation."

Cheats

Use these when students ask the obvious questions

These answers keep the discussion tied to the page instead of drifting into abstract agent theory.

If they ask "where is the harness?"

Answer

The page is the harness surface. The cards and modals expose the harness components.

If they ask "is this automation?"

Answer

Parts are automation-ready: SKILL.md downloads, prompt packets, and harness levers.

If they ask "what runs?"

Answer

In this lesson, the run is the controlled walkthrough. In implementation, each skill or harness would execute work.

If they ask "what proves it worked?"

Answer

Proof is explicit: Verify, Evidence, Trace, Trust, Proof Authority, and release readiness.

If they ask about agents

Answer

The build primer says not to start with agents. Start with work, stages, workers, contracts, and gates.

If they get lost in cards

Answer

Group them into two layers: orchestrator harnesses and model-call harnesses.

If they ask why skills matter

Answer

Skills package repeatable behavior. That is where lesson content becomes development automation.

If time is short

Answer

Teach only Proof Authority, Tool Registry, Model Router, Prompt, Verify, Evidence, and Trust.

Student exercise

Make students translate one visible card into a harness design

This exercise uses `/welcomeback/` directly. Students do not invent a random workflow; they pick a card from the page.

Instructions

Give this prompt

Say: "Pick one card on the `/welcomeback` page. It can be Proof Authority, Tool Registry, Model Router, Prompt, Verify, Trust, or anything else. Translate that card into setup, run, observe, judge, and recover."

  • Give students five minutes to pick and draft.
  • Ask three students to share different cards.
  • Challenge any answer that does not include evidence or recovery.
Worksheet for /welcomeback card 
Card chosen:

What work does this harness control?

Setup:
  What state, context, policy, or dependency must exist first?

Run:
  What action does the harness allow, route, trigger, or inspect?

Observe:
  What evidence, trace, status, metric, or artifact should it capture?

Judge:
  What makes the result trusted, blocked, reviewed, or expired?

Recover:
  What happens when the route fails, stalls, violates policy, or needs rollback?

Performance lever:

Cost lever:

Capability lever:

One SKILL.md or automation this could become:

Model answers

Examples you can use if the room needs help

These show students what a strong translation from page card to harness design sounds like.

Proof Authority

Judgment harness

Setup: define evidence types and sufficiency thresholds.

Run: score evidence before work is trusted.

Observe: collect proof bundles, crosschecks, and waivers.

Judge: trusted, insufficient, waived, or blocked.

Recover: request more evidence or route to review.

Tool Registry

Permission harness

Setup: know allowed tools, credentials, dry-run rules, and reversibility.

Run: execute only approved tool actions.

Observe: log commands, outputs, and side effects.

Judge: allowed, needs approval, denied, or unsafe.

Recover: rollback, stop, or escalate.

Model Router

Routing harness

Setup: model catalog, policy, cost limits, fallback chain.

Run: route the task to a provider/model path.

Observe: record route, latency, cost, output, and failures.

Judge: adequate route, fallback needed, or blocked.

Recover: retry, fallback, downgrade, or ask for approval.

Close

Closing script for this specific page

End by tying the visual system back to the durable harness model.

Say: "What you saw on `/welcomeback` is a command-center version of harness thinking. It exposes state, names dependencies, separates control planes, packages capabilities as skills, opens details for inspection, and puts proof, gates, trust, fallback, and recovery on the surface."

Final line: "Harness engineering is the craft of making complex work inspectable enough that automation can be trusted."
